title: Spanish wave marbling
subtitle: The dragged-comb wave pattern that characterises the Spanish or Old Dutch marble
excerpt: The Spanish wave marble is made in two stages: a nonpareil base of finely combed parallel lines, then a second pass with a wide comb dragged in the opposite direction in a slow wave movement. The result is a flowing, rhythmic pattern associated with eighteenth-century bookbinding.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (English translation 1894), chapter on combed patterns including the Spanish wave. C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881 edition). Both public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: A closely combed pattern of fine parallel lines worked in two directions, forming the foundation for many combed marble patterns.
A marbling pattern made by dragging a wide comb slowly across a nonpareil base in a flowing S-curve motion.
The carrageenan-thickened water surface in a marbling tray on which pigments float.

content.text: The
spanish-wave
marble requires a well-prepared
nonpareil base
. The nonpareil must be combed evenly and the pigment must be spread consistently. The wave pass amplifies any irregularity in the base, so the base must be right before the wave begins.

Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing the mordant solution; rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan size is non-toxic but spills are slippery: wipe up immediately.
What you need
Size: carrageenan prepared the previous day, skimmed to remove surface bubbles. The size must be at room temperature.
Pigments: at least three colours of liquid marbling acrylic, each with 1 to 2 drops of ox-gall added per teaspoon of colour. Test drop behaviour on the size before beginning.
Combs: a fine-tooth comb with 5 mm tooth spacing for the nonpareil pass; a wide-tooth comb with 15 to 20 mm tooth spacing for the wave pass.
Building the nonpareil base
Drop alternating colours across the
size
surface until it is covered in a roughly even layer.
Draw the fine-tooth comb from left to right across the full width of the tray in a single smooth pass. Work at a consistent pace.
Draw the fine-tooth comb from right to left, offset by half a tooth spacing. The result is a fine chevron or nonpareil pattern covering the full surface.
Working the Spanish wave
Hold the wide-tooth comb at one short end of the tray, teeth pointing down into the nonpareil.
Draw the comb along the length of the tray at a slow, even pace, moving the comb gently up and down in a flowing wave as it travels. The wave should be about 30 to 40 mm in amplitude.
The wave distorts the nonpareil into the characteristic flowing S-curves of the Spanish marble.
Lay the mordanted sheet and lift in the normal way.
Troubleshooting

attrs.symptom: Wave pattern is irregular and the curves vary in size
attrs.cause: The comb moved at an uneven pace or the wave amplitude changed during the pass.

content.text: Practise the wave motion in the air before the comb touches the size. The arm movement is consistent throughout; the pace and amplitude are set before beginning.
